bullets: 3 Star Destroyers, 3 Assault Carriers, 12 TIE Fighters
12 Stormtroopers, 5 AT-STs, 1 AT-AT, 1 Death Star
Place in any Imperial loyal or subjugated systems
At least 1 ground unit must be in each Imperial system

summary.title: Place Rebel Starting Units
bullets: 1 Corellian Corvette, 1 Rebel Transport, 2 X-wings, 2 Y-wings
6 Rebel Troopers, 2 Airspeeders
Place on the Rebel Base space and/or any 1 Rebel or neutral system

summary.title: Draw Starting Action Cards
bullets: Each player draws 2 random starting action cards (no recruit icon)
Place facedown near faction sheet; keep hidden from opponent
Return unused starting action cards to box without revealing

summary.title: Choose Rebel Base Location
bullets: Rebel player secretly selects 1 probe card from the deck
Place facedown under the Rebel Base Location space
Do NOT reveal to the Imperial player
Shuffle remaining probe deck and place on Probe Deck space

summary.title: Draw Starting Mission Hand
bullets: Each player takes their 4 starting missions into hand
Draw 2 more from mission deck for a starting hand of 6
Starting missions (yellow arrow) can never be discarded
Rebel player takes the first turn in the Assignment Phase

walkthrough.title: Game Overview and Victory Conditions
walkthrough.text: Star Wars: Rebellion is an asymmetric two-player game played over a series of game rounds. Each round has three phases: Assignment Phase, Command Phase, and Refresh Phase. The two factions win in completely different ways. The Imperial player wins by finding the Rebel base and destroying all Rebel units in that system. The base must be revealed first, which happens when Imperial ground units enter the system. The Rebel player wins by gaining enough reputation that the reputation marker reaches the time marker on the time track. The reputation marker starts on space 14 and moves toward the time marker, while the time marker advances one space each round. If they ever share the same space, the Rebels win immediately.

walkthrough.title: Assignment Phase
walkthrough.text: The Assignment Phase is the first phase of each round. Players assign leaders to missions from their hand. To assign a leader, take a mission card from your hand and place it facedown near your faction sheet. Then place one or two leaders from your leader pool on top of that card. The Rebel player assigns all of their leaders to missions first, then the Imperial player does the same. Mission cards stay facedown and hidden during this phase. Any leaders not assigned to missions stay in the leader pool. Leaders in the pool can still activate systems, oppose missions, or join combats during the Command Phase.

walkthrough.title: Command Phase: Overview
walkthrough.text: During the Command Phase, players alternate taking turns. The Rebel player always goes first. On your turn, you have three options: activate a system using a leader from your leader pool, reveal a mission that one of your leaders is assigned to, or pass. Once you pass, you cannot take any more turns this Command Phase, but you can still use leaders from your pool to oppose missions or add them to combat. Turns alternate between the Rebel and Imperial player until both have passed. Any leaders still on unrevealed mission cards return to their leader pool, and those mission cards go back to the player's hand.

walkthrough.title: Activating a System
walkthrough.text: To activate a system, take a leader from your leader pool and place it in any system on the board. The leader must have at least one tactic value to activate a system. After placing the leader, you may move your units from adjacent systems into the activated system. Ships move freely between adjacent systems. Ground units and TIE Fighters require transport capacity from ships moving from the same system. After movement, if Imperial ground units entered a system, the Rebel player must declare whether the Rebel base is there. If so, the base is revealed. Finally, if both factions have units in the same theater, combat occurs. After combat, if there is an Imperial ground unit in a system without Imperial loyalty, place a subjugation marker there.

walkthrough.title: Movement Rules
walkthrough.text: When you activate a system, you can move units from multiple adjacent systems into the activated system. Ships move freely. Ground units and TIE Fighters need transport capacity, which is provided by certain ships. Each ground unit or TIE Fighter takes up one capacity. Units with the transport restriction icon can only move when accompanied by transport-capable ships from the same system. Units cannot move out of a system that already contains a friendly leader. Two systems that share a border are adjacent. Units can cross the orange region borders but cannot pass through impassable areas shown in light red. Immobile units like the Death Star Under Construction and structures cannot move at all.

walkthrough.title: Revealing a Mission
walkthrough.text: To reveal a mission, flip one of your assigned mission cards faceup and read it aloud. The leaders assigned to it must have combined skill icons matching the mission's requirement. Place those leaders in the system specified by the card. If the card says Resolve, skip straight to performing the ability. If it says Attempt, the mission can be opposed. Your opponent may send one leader from their pool to the system. Then both sides roll dice equal to their leaders' matching skill icons. Each hit or direct hit is one success. Each special result is two successes. If the attempter gets more successes, the mission succeeds and the ability is performed. Otherwise it fails. Starting missions return to your hand after use. All other missions are discarded.

walkthrough.title: Opposing Missions
walkthrough.text: When a mission is attempted, the opponent may send one leader from their leader pool to the system to oppose it. If there is at least one opposing leader in the system, the mission is opposed and both players roll dice. Each player rolls dice equal to the total matching skill icons on all their leaders in the system. If a mission card has a leader portrait in the top-left corner, the owner gains two bonus successes if one of their assigned leaders matches that portrait. Even if the opposing leader has no matching skill icons, the attempter still needs at least one success to succeed. A captured leader does not participate in opposing missions unless the mission is specifically attempted against that captured leader.

walkthrough.title: Combat: Setup and Tactic Cards
walkthrough.text: Combat occurs when both factions have units in the same theater within a system. Combat has multiple rounds. First, if a player does not have a leader with tactic values in the system, they may add one from their leader pool. Then each player draws space tactic cards equal to their leader's space tactic value, and ground tactic cards equal to their leader's ground tactic value. If you have multiple leaders, use the highest value from each theater. You only draw space tactic cards if both sides have ships, and ground tactic cards if both sides have ground units. The current player draws first.

walkthrough.title: Combat Round: Space and Ground Battles
walkthrough.text: Each combat round proceeds through four steps. First, the Space Battle: the current player resolves one attack with all ships, then the opponent does the same. Second, the Ground Battle: same process but with ground units. Third, Retreat: starting with the current player, each player may choose to retreat. Fourth, check if both factions still have units in the same theater. If so, another combat round begins. If not, combat ends. Players only resolve the space battle step if both sides have ships, and the ground battle step if both sides have ground units.

walkthrough.title: Resolving an Attack
walkthrough.text: To resolve an attack, roll dice matching the color and quantity of your units' attack values. Each player can roll a maximum of five black dice and five red dice per attack. After rolling, you may perform combat actions in any order: spend a die showing a special icon to draw a tactic card from the matching theater deck, or play a tactic card from your hand. Some tactic cards require spending a special die to play. After combat actions, assign your hit and direct hit dice as damage to enemy units. Hits on red dice can only damage units with red health. Hits on black dice can only damage units with black health. Direct hits can damage any unit. Then the opponent may play tactic cards to block damage. Finally, units with damage equal to or exceeding their health are destroyed.

walkthrough.title: Retreating from Combat
walkthrough.text: During the retreat step, starting with the current player, each player may choose to retreat. To retreat, take one of your leaders from the system and place it in an adjacent system. Then move your units to that leader's system following normal movement and transport rules. You must retreat to a system containing your units or loyalty markers if possible. You cannot retreat to a system with enemy units, or to the system the opponent moved from to initiate combat. All ships must leave when retreating, but you can leave ground units and TIE Fighters behind. If you leave units behind and the opponent still has units in that theater, another combat round occurs. The Imperial player cannot retreat if they have a Death Star in the combat.

walkthrough.title: The Rebel Base
walkthrough.text: The Rebel base is secretly placed during setup. While hidden, Rebel units on the Rebel Base space can move to the base's actual system or adjacent systems. Rebels can also move units to the Rebel Base space from the base's system or adjacent systems. The base is revealed when Imperial ground units enter its system, or optionally by the Rebel player during their Command Phase turn. When revealed, all units and leaders on the Rebel Base space are placed in the system shown on the probe card. The Rapid Mobilization mission allows the Rebel player to establish a new base by drawing four probe cards and selecting a new location.

walkthrough.title: Probe Cards and Finding the Base
walkthrough.text: The probe deck contains one card for each system except Coruscant. During each Refresh Phase, the Imperial player draws two probe cards. These let the Imperial player narrow down where the Rebel base is NOT. If you draw a probe card for a system, the base cannot be there. The Imperial player keeps these cards hidden from the Rebel player. Several Imperial missions also help locate the base, such as Gather Intel and Long Range Probe. The process of elimination is a key Imperial strategy: the more probe cards you draw, the fewer possible locations remain for the hidden base.

walkthrough.title: Loyalty and Subjugation
walkthrough.text: Each populous system can have a loyalty marker showing which faction controls it. Loyalty lets you build units from a system's resources and deploy units there. When you gain loyalty in a neutral system, place your loyalty marker. Gaining one loyalty in an enemy system removes their marker, making it neutral. Gaining two loyalty in an enemy system flips it to yours. Coruscant is always Imperial and cannot change loyalty. Subjugation occurs when Imperial ground units occupy a system without Imperial loyalty. A subjugation marker is placed, letting the Empire build from the first resource icon and deploy there. Subjugation is removed if all Imperial ground units leave.

walkthrough.title: Objective Cards and Reputation
walkthrough.text: The Rebel player gains reputation by fulfilling objective cards. Each card specifies a condition and when it can be played: during combat or at the start of the Refresh Phase. Only one objective can be played per combat and per Refresh Phase. When you gain reputation, move the reputation marker one space toward the time marker. The objective deck is structured in stages: Stage I objectives are easiest and drawn first, with Stage II and III becoming available as the game progresses. The Rebel player draws one new objective card each Refresh Phase. Objective cards do not count toward the mission hand limit and are kept hidden from the Imperial player.

walkthrough.title: Refresh Phase
walkthrough.text: The Refresh Phase has six steps performed in order. First, retrieve all leaders from the game board back to leader pools. The Rebel player may play one Start of Refresh Phase objective card before this step. Second, each player draws two mission cards, then discards down to 10 if over the limit. Third, the Imperial player draws two probe cards. Fourth, the Rebel player draws one objective card. Fifth, advance the time marker one space. If the new space has a recruit icon, each player draws two action cards and recruits one leader. If it has a build icon, players simultaneously build units by placing them on their build queues. Sixth, starting with the Rebel player, slide all units down the build queue one space and deploy any that slide off.

walkthrough.title: Building and Deploying Units
walkthrough.text: When a build icon appears on the time track, each player builds one unit per resource icon in their loyal and subjugated systems. Units are placed on the build queue at the space matching the number next to the resource icon. For subjugated systems, only the first resource icon is used. During step 6 of the Refresh Phase, all units slide one space down the queue. Units that slide off are deployed to any loyal or subjugated system, with a maximum of two units per system. The Rebel player can also deploy to the Rebel Base space while it is hidden. Units cannot be deployed to systems with sabotage markers or enemy units.

walkthrough.title: Recruiting Leaders
walkthrough.text: When the time marker reaches a space with a recruit icon, each player draws two cards from their action deck. Choose one card and recruit the leader shown on it, placing that leader in your leader pool. Keep the chosen action card facedown. Place the unchosen card on the bottom of your action deck without revealing it. The Rebel player declares their recruitment first. Each action card has a powerful one-time ability tied to the leader shown on it. These abilities can be used during missions, combat, or the Assignment Phase depending on the card's timing text. Once used, the card is returned to the game box.

walkthrough.title: The Death Star
walkthrough.text: The Death Star is an immensely powerful unit. It follows ship rules but cannot be assigned or dealt damage through normal combat. The Imperial player cannot retreat if a Death Star is in the combat. The Death Star Under Construction is immobile. If it is destroyed, the completed Death Star on the build queue is also destroyed. The Rebel player can destroy a Death Star by revealing a Death Star Plans objective card during combat. If there is at least one Rebel fighter in the system, they roll three dice. If at least one direct hit is rolled, the Death Star is destroyed and the Rebel player gains reputation. A second Death Star can be built using the Construct Death Star mission.

walkthrough.title: Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Action cards provide one-time abilities for specific leaders. Each has a timing keyword: Assignment means play it instead of assigning a leader during the Assignment Phase. Start of Combat means play it after step 1 of combat. Immediate means it must be used as soon as gained. Special means follow the card's instructions. To use an action card during a mission or combat, one of the leaders shown on the card must be in that system. After use, the card is returned to the game box. You can look at your own action cards at any time but cannot see your opponent's. These are some of the most powerful effects in the game and can swing critical moments.

summary.title: Game Overview and Victory Conditions
bullets: Asymmetric 2-player game played over multiple rounds
Each round: Assignment Phase, Command Phase, Refresh Phase
Empire wins: Imperial units in Rebel base's system with no Rebel units remaining (base must be revealed)
Rebels win: reputation marker reaches the time marker on the time track

summary.title: Assignment Phase
bullets: Players assign leaders from their pool to facedown mission cards
1 or 2 leaders per mission; unassigned leaders stay in pool
Rebel player assigns first, then Imperial player
Mission cards stay hidden during this phase

summary.title: Command Phase: Overview
bullets: Players alternate turns; Rebel player goes first
Three options: activate a system, reveal a mission, or pass
Passing ends your Command Phase turns, but you can still oppose missions and add leaders to combat
Unrevealed missions return to hand at end of phase

summary.title: Activating a System
bullets: Place a leader (must have tactic values) from pool into any system
Move units from adjacent systems into the activated system
If Imperial ground units enter: Rebel must declare if base is there
Combat occurs if both factions have units in the same theater; subjugate after combat if applicable

summary.title: Movement Rules
bullets: Ships move freely between adjacent systems
Ground units and TIE Fighters require transport capacity from ships
Units cannot move out of a system containing a friendly leader
Immobile units (Death Star Under Construction, structures) cannot move

summary.title: Revealing a Mission
bullets: Flip mission card faceup; leaders must meet skill requirement
Resolve missions skip opposition; Attempt missions can be opposed
Both sides roll dice equal to matching skill icons; hits = 1 success, specials = 2
Starting missions return to hand; all others are discarded

summary.title: Opposing Missions
bullets: Opponent may send 1 leader from pool to the system
Both players roll dice for matching skill icons
Leader portrait on card grants 2 bonus successes if matching leader is assigned
Attempter needs more successes than opponent to succeed

summary.title: Combat: Setup and Tactic Cards
bullets: Players may add 1 leader from pool if none with tactic values in system
Draw space tactic cards = leader's space tactic value
Draw ground tactic cards = leader's ground tactic value
Multiple leaders: use highest value per theater

summary.title: Combat Round: Space and Ground Battles
bullets: Step I: Space Battle — current player attacks with ships, then opponent
Step II: Ground Battle — current player attacks with ground, then opponent
Step III: Retreat — each player may retreat
Step IV: If both factions still have units in same theater, new combat round

summary.title: Resolving an Attack
bullets: Roll dice matching units' attack values (max 5 black + 5 red per attack)
Combat actions: spend special die to draw tactic card, or play tactic card
Assign damage: red hits to red health, black hits to black health, direct hits to any
Opponent blocks damage with tactic cards; units at full damage are destroyed

summary.title: Retreating from Combat
bullets: Place 1 leader in adjacent system; move units there following normal rules
Must retreat to a system with your units or loyalty if possible
All ships must leave; ground units and TIE Fighters may stay behind
Empire cannot retreat if Death Star is in the combat

summary.title: The Rebel Base
bullets: Hidden base: Rebel units move to/from base space and adjacent systems
Revealed when Imperial ground units enter the system
All units on Rebel Base space deploy to the system when revealed
Rapid Mobilization mission lets Rebels establish a new base

summary.title: Probe Cards and Finding the Base
bullets: Imperial draws 2 probe cards each Refresh Phase
Each probe card eliminates one possible base location
Imperial missions like Gather Intel and Long Range Probe help narrow search
Process of elimination is key Imperial strategy

summary.title: Loyalty and Subjugation
bullets: Loyalty lets you build from and deploy to a system
+1 loyalty in enemy system removes their marker; +2 flips it to yours
Coruscant is always Imperial; cannot change
Subjugation: Imperial ground units in non-loyal system; build from 1st resource icon only

summary.title: Objective Cards and Reputation
bullets: Rebels gain reputation by fulfilling objective card conditions
Play timing: during combat or at Start of Refresh Phase (1 per timing window)
Stages I, II, III — progressively harder but worth more reputation
Rebel draws 1 objective per Refresh Phase; no hand limit for objectives

summary.title: Refresh Phase
bullets: 1. Retrieve leaders; 2. Draw 2 missions (discard to 10); 3. Draw 2 probe cards (Imperial)
4. Draw 1 objective (Rebel); 5. Advance time marker — recruit/build if icons shown
6. Slide build queue down 1 space; deploy units that slide off (max 2 per system)
Rebel player may play Start of Refresh Phase objective before step 1

summary.title: Building and Deploying Units
bullets: Build 1 unit per resource icon in loyal/subjugated systems (subjugated = 1st icon only)
Units placed on build queue at the matching number space
Units slide down each Refresh Phase; deploy when they slide off
Deploy to loyal/subjugated systems (max 2 per system); Rebels can deploy to hidden base space

summary.title: Recruiting Leaders
bullets: Draw 2 action cards when time marker hits recruit icon
Choose 1 card: recruit its leader into your pool, keep card for its ability
Unchosen card goes to bottom of action deck unrevealed
Rebel player declares recruitment first

summary.title: The Death Star
bullets: Cannot be damaged through normal combat; Empire cannot retreat with it
Death Star Under Construction is immobile; if destroyed, completed DS on queue is also destroyed
Rebels destroy DS with Death Star Plans objective: need 1 fighter, roll 3 dice, need 1 direct hit
Construct Death Star mission builds a second Death Star

summary.title: Action Cards
bullets: One-time abilities tied to specific leaders; returned to box after use
Timing keywords: Assignment, Start of Combat, Immediate, Special
Leader shown on card must be in the system to use during mission/combat
Powerful game-swinging effects — use strategically at critical moments

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Galactic Civil War
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with two players: one controlling the Rebel Alliance and one controlling the Galactic Empire. We will cover the Assignment Phase, several Command Phase turns, and touch on the Refresh Phase. Follow along with the moves, and by the end you will have seen all the core mechanics in action. Ready? The galaxy awaits.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: The board is set up. The Empire has forces spread across five systems with loyalty and subjugation. The Death Star looms in orbit. The Rebel player has secretly placed the base and has most of their small fleet on the Rebel Base space. Both players have their four starting leaders in their leader pools and six mission cards in hand. The reputation marker sits on space 14 and the time marker on space 1. The Empire needs to find and crush the base. The Rebels need to survive and gain reputation.

walkthrough.title: Assignment Phase: Rebel Assigns Missions
walkthrough.text: The Rebel player goes first. Looking at their hand, they decide to assign two missions. They place the Seek Yoda mission card facedown and put Mon Mothma on it — she has the diplomacy icons needed. Then they place a second mission facedown and assign Princess Leia. Rieekan and Dodonna stay in the leader pool for activating systems or opposing Imperial missions. The Rebel player signals they are done assigning.

walkthrough.title: Assignment Phase: Empire Assigns Missions
walkthrough.text: Now the Imperial player assigns. They place the Gather Intel mission facedown with Colonel Yularen on it — his intel icons match the requirement. They also assign Darth Vader to the Capture Rebel Operative mission. Emperor Palpatine and General Tagge remain in the leader pool. The Imperial player is done. Notice both players assigned only two of their four leaders, keeping two flexible for the Command Phase.

walkthrough.title: Command Phase Turn 1: Rebel Reveals a Mission
walkthrough.text: The Rebel player takes the first Command Phase turn. They choose to reveal the Seek Yoda mission. They flip the card faceup and place Mon Mothma in the Dagobah system. This mission says Resolve, not Attempt, so the Imperial player cannot oppose it. The Rebel player resolves the effect, gaining a strategic advantage. Mon Mothma is now in Dagobah on the board and will stay there until the Refresh Phase retrieves her.

walkthrough.title: Command Phase Turn 2: Empire Activates a System
walkthrough.text: The Imperial player's turn. Instead of revealing a mission, they decide to activate a system. They take General Tagge from the leader pool and place him in the Mandalore system. Tagge has tactic values, so he can activate. The Imperial player moves a Star Destroyer and 3 TIE Fighters from an adjacent system into Mandalore, along with 4 Stormtroopers using the Star Destroyer's transport capacity. There are no Rebel units here, so no combat occurs. Since there is now an Imperial ground unit in a system without Imperial loyalty, a subjugation marker is placed.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Good Imperial Move
walkthrough.text: Activating a system to subjugate it serves two purposes for the Empire. First, the Empire can now build units from that system's first resource icon during the Refresh Phase. Second, the Empire is spreading its presence across the board, which limits where the Rebel base could be hiding and makes it easier to project force. Early subjugation is critical for the Empire because more systems mean more units built each round, compounding their military advantage over time.

walkthrough.title: Command Phase Turn 3: Rebel Activates a System
walkthrough.text: The Rebel player takes General Rieekan from the pool and activates the Rebel Base space. They move their Corellian Corvette, 2 X-wings, and 3 Rebel Troopers from the Rebel Base space toward an adjacent system to establish a visible presence. The Rebel player wants the Empire to see units in this system, drawing attention away from where the base actually is. This is classic Rebel misdirection.

walkthrough.title: Command Phase Turn 4: Empire Reveals Gather Intel
walkthrough.text: The Imperial player reveals the Gather Intel mission. Yularen is placed in a system with Rebel units. This mission is an Attempt mission, so the Rebel player can oppose it. The Rebel player decides to send Dodonna from their leader pool to oppose. Yularen has 2 intel icons matching the mission, so the Imperial rolls 2 dice. Dodonna has 1 intel icon, so the Rebel rolls 1 die. The Imperial rolls a hit and a special — that is 3 successes. The Rebel rolls a blank — 0 successes. The mission succeeds. The Imperial player draws probe cards based on Rebel units in the system, narrowing down base locations.

walkthrough.title: The Empire's Mistake: Overcommitting to One Lead
walkthrough.text: Feeling confident after Gather Intel succeeded, the Imperial player decides to go all-in. They activate Emperor Palpatine and send a massive fleet to the system where they think the Rebel base might be. Three Star Destroyers, 6 TIE Fighters, and 8 Stormtroopers rumble in. The Rebel player calmly declares: the base is not here. All those units moved for nothing. The Emperor is now stuck on the board in an empty system, unavailable for missions next round. The Empire wasted their most powerful leader and a huge fleet movement on a guess.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Do Not Guess Blindly
walkthrough.text: The takeaway: the Empire should never make a big military push toward the Rebel base without strong evidence. Use probe cards and intel missions to eliminate possibilities first. Every probe card you draw removes one system from consideration. Wait until you have narrowed it down to two or three possibilities before committing forces. Guessing wrong costs you tempo because your leaders and units are out of position, and the Rebels get a free round to score objectives.

walkthrough.title: The Empire's Second Mistake: Ignoring Objectives
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, the Rebel player has been quietly fulfilling an objective. At the start of the Refresh Phase, they reveal a Stage I objective card: they meet the condition of having Rebel loyalty in a certain number of systems. They gain 2 reputation, moving the marker from space 14 to space 12. The Imperial player was so focused on finding the base that they never tried to remove Rebel loyalty or put pressure on Rebel systems. The reputation marker just got two spaces closer to winning for the Rebels.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Empire Must Apply Pressure Everywhere
walkthrough.text: The Empire cannot just hunt for the base. They must also prevent the Rebel player from fulfilling objectives. This means removing Rebel loyalty through diplomacy missions, subjugating neutral systems, and maintaining military presence across the board. If you let the Rebels freely accumulate objectives, the reputation marker will reach the time marker before you ever find the base. Think of the Empire as needing to do two things at once: search and suppress.

walkthrough.title: Refresh Phase Walkthrough
walkthrough.text: Now both players have passed. The Refresh Phase begins. Step 1: All leaders return to leader pools. Mon Mothma comes back from Dagobah, Tagge from Mandalore, everyone returns. Step 2: Each player draws 2 mission cards. Step 3: The Imperial player draws 2 probe cards, secretly eliminating more base possibilities. Step 4: The Rebel player draws 1 objective card. Step 5: Advance the time marker from space 1 to space 2. Check the icon — if there is a build icon, both players build units. If there is a recruit icon, both players draw action cards and recruit a new leader.

walkthrough.title: Building Units During Refresh
walkthrough.text: Assume space 2 has a build icon. The Imperial player builds one unit for each resource icon across their loyal and subjugated systems. With five controlled systems, that could be six to eight new units placed on the build queue. The Rebel player builds from their three loyal systems plus the Rebel Base space. Fewer units total, but they build efficiently. Units are placed on the queue and will slide down each round until they deploy. The Empire's production advantage is massive — the Rebels must use missions and objectives to compensate.

walkthrough.title: Deploying and the Build Queue
walkthrough.text: In step 6 of the Refresh Phase, all units slide one space down the build queue. Any that fall off the end are deployed. You can deploy up to 2 units per loyal or subjugated system. The Rebel player can also deploy to the hidden Rebel Base space. Units that cannot deploy stay on the 1 space of the queue. Plan your deployments carefully: you want units arriving where you need them. The Rebel player should deploy to the Rebel Base space when possible to keep forces concentrated and hidden.

walkthrough.title: Rebel Player's Smart Move: Scoring Objectives
walkthrough.text: Let us rewind to what the Rebel player did right this round. They used Mon Mothma for a safe Resolve mission that could not be opposed. They used Rieekan to create a decoy fleet drawing Imperial attention. They kept Leia in reserve on a mission card they never revealed — holding it for next round when conditions might be better. And they scored an objective at the start of Refresh. That is four effective actions from four leaders. The Rebel player is playing efficiently and staying unpredictable.

walkthrough.title: Continue Into Round 2
walkthrough.text: Round 2 begins with a new Assignment Phase. Both players now have new mission cards, potentially a new leader, and fresh units being built. The Imperial player should shift strategy: use intel missions to draw probe cards, use diplomacy to flip Rebel loyalty, and position forces near suspected base locations without overcommitting. The Rebel player should keep scoring objectives, protect loyal systems, and stay unpredictable with base movement if available. Each round, the time marker advances — the Rebels are one step closer to winning.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen the Assignment Phase in action, multiple Command Phase options including activating systems and revealing missions, an opposed mission roll, combat setup, Imperial mistakes of guessing blindly and ignoring objectives, and the full Refresh Phase. You have watched both sides make strategic choices that define the Galactic Civil War. Remember: the Empire must search methodically while applying pressure. The Rebels must stay hidden, score objectives, and use misdirection. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin Your Rebellion
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for beginner strategy for both factions and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every unit, leader, mission type, system, and game term.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. May the Force be with you.

summary.title: Welcome to the Galactic Civil War
bullets: Scripted first-round tutorial with 2 players: Rebel Alliance and Galactic Empire
Covers Assignment Phase, Command Phase turns, and Refresh Phase
Follow along to see all core mechanics in action

summary.title: Starting Positions
bullets: Empire has forces in 5 systems with loyalty/subjugation and the Death Star
Rebels have most units hidden on the Rebel Base space
Reputation on 14, time marker on 1 — the clock is ticking for both sides

summary.title: Assignment Phase: Rebel Assigns Missions
bullets: Rebel assigns Mon Mothma to Seek Yoda mission
Assigns Leia to a second facedown mission
Rieekan and Dodonna stay in pool for flexibility

summary.title: Assignment Phase: Empire Assigns Missions
bullets: Empire assigns Yularen to Gather Intel (intel match)
Assigns Vader to Capture Rebel Operative
Palpatine and Tagge stay in pool — balanced approach

summary.title: Command Phase Turn 1: Rebel Reveals a Mission
bullets: Rebel reveals Seek Yoda — a Resolve mission (cannot be opposed)
Mon Mothma placed in Dagobah; effect resolved immediately
Mon Mothma stays on the board until Refresh Phase

summary.title: Command Phase Turn 2: Empire Activates a System
bullets: Tagge activates Mandalore; ships and ground units move in
No Rebel units present — no combat
Subjugation marker placed — Empire can now build from this system

summary.title: Why That Was a Good Imperial Move
bullets: Subjugation = more resource icons = more units built each round
Spreading presence limits where the base could be
Early subjugation compounds the Empire's military advantage

summary.title: Command Phase Turn 3: Rebel Activates a System
bullets: Rieekan moves units from Rebel Base space to a visible system
Creates a decoy fleet — draws Imperial attention away from the real base
Classic Rebel misdirection strategy

summary.title: Command Phase Turn 4: Empire Reveals Gather Intel
bullets: Yularen attempts Gather Intel — Rebel sends Dodonna to oppose
Imperial rolls 2 dice (2 intel icons) vs Rebel's 1 die
Imperial succeeds with 3 vs 0 — draws probe cards to narrow search

summary.title: The Empire's Mistake: Overcommitting to One Lead
bullets: Empire sends Palpatine and massive fleet to a guessed base location
Rebel declares: base is NOT here
Wasted the Emperor and fleet position on an empty system

summary.title: Lesson: Do Not Guess Blindly
bullets: Never commit major forces without strong probe card evidence
Use probe cards and intel missions to eliminate possibilities first
Wrong guesses cost tempo — leaders out of position, Rebels score freely

summary.title: The Empire's Second Mistake: Ignoring Objectives
bullets: Rebel scores a Stage I objective at start of Refresh — gains 2 reputation
Reputation moves from 14 to 12 while Empire focused only on base hunting
Empire never tried to remove Rebel loyalty or suppress objectives

summary.title: Lesson: The Empire Must Apply Pressure Everywhere
bullets: Empire must search for the base AND prevent Rebel objectives
Remove Rebel loyalty, subjugate neutral systems, maintain military presence
Letting Rebels score freely means the reputation marker wins before you find the base

summary.title: Refresh Phase Walkthrough
bullets: Step 1: All leaders return to pools; Step 2: Draw 2 missions each
Step 3: Imperial draws 2 probe cards; Step 4: Rebel draws 1 objective
Step 5: Advance time marker — recruit/build if icons shown

summary.title: Building Units During Refresh
bullets: Build 1 unit per resource icon in loyal/subjugated systems
Empire builds many more units than Rebels due to more controlled systems
Units placed on build queue; slide down each round until deployed

summary.title: Deploying and the Build Queue
bullets: Units slide 1 space down queue each Refresh; deploy when they fall off
Max 2 units deployed per system; Rebels can deploy to hidden base space
Plan deployments to get units where you need them

summary.title: Rebel Player's Smart Move: Scoring Objectives
bullets: Mon Mothma: safe Resolve mission; Rieekan: decoy fleet; Leia: held in reserve
Scored objective at start of Refresh — 4 effective actions from 4 leaders
Efficient, unpredictable play is the Rebel way

summary.title: Continue Into Round 2
bullets: New round: fresh missions, potential new leader, units building
Empire should shift to intel + diplomacy + positioned forces
Rebels keep scoring, protect loyalty, stay unpredictable

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have seen Assignment, Command, and Refresh phases in action
Watched missions, system activation, opposition rolls, and strategic mistakes
Empire must search methodically + suppress; Rebels must hide + score + misdirect

summary.title: Before You Begin Your Rebellion
bullets: Check out General Tips for beginner strategy for both factions
Reference the Appendix for every unit, leader, mission type, and term
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes for Both Sides
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common beginner mistakes. For the Empire: guessing the base location without enough probe card evidence, ignoring Rebel objectives and letting reputation snowball, and spreading forces too thin without a plan. For the Rebels: placing the base in an obvious location like a Rebel loyal system, moving units from the base space and creating a trail back to it, and holding objective cards too long waiting for perfect conditions instead of scoring when available. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps on both sides.

walkthrough.title: Imperial Opening Priorities
walkthrough.text: As the Empire, your first few rounds should focus on three things. First, subjugate systems aggressively. Every system you control adds resources to your build queue, and the Empire's production advantage is its greatest weapon. Second, use intel missions like Gather Intel to draw probe cards and narrow down the base location. Third, position your forces so they can reach multiple suspected base locations quickly. Do not chase the base yet. Build up, gather intelligence, and strike when you have confidence. The Empire can afford to be patient because its military advantage grows every round.

walkthrough.title: Rebel Opening Priorities
walkthrough.text: As the Rebels, your early game is about survival and setup. Focus on diplomacy missions to gain loyalty in systems, which fuels your ability to build units and score objectives. Use safe Resolve missions whenever possible to avoid risky dice rolls. Keep your base hidden by avoiding suspicious movement patterns. Establish a small visible presence in one or two systems to create decoys. Most importantly, look at your objective cards and plan how to fulfill them. The Rebels win by scoring objectives, not by winning battles. Every action should be moving you closer to reputation gains.

walkthrough.title: Leader Assignment Strategy
walkthrough.text: How you split leaders between missions and the pool is one of the most important decisions each round. Assign leaders to missions only when you have strong targets and the right skill matches. Keep at least one leader with good tactic values in the pool for activating systems and responding to threats. For the Empire, Vader is excellent on spec ops missions but also draws great tactic cards in combat. For the Rebels, Leia's diplomacy makes her ideal for loyalty missions, but she is also useful opposing Imperial intel missions. Never assign all four leaders to missions — you need pool leaders for flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Time Pressure
walkthrough.text: The time marker advances one space each Refresh Phase. The reputation marker starts at 14. This means the Rebels have roughly 14 rounds to win, but good Rebel play can close that gap much faster. The Empire must balance long-term military buildup with short-term pressure to slow Rebel reputation gains. If the reputation marker reaches 10 or below and you have not narrowed the base to two or three systems, the Empire is in serious trouble. Conversely, if the Rebels have not gained any reputation by round 4, they need to take more risks with objective scoring.

walkthrough.title: Combat Is a Means, Not an End
walkthrough.text: New players often seek out combat, but in Rebellion, combat is expensive. Leaders get tied down, units are destroyed, and tactic cards are spent. Fight only when it serves your strategic goals. For the Empire, combat matters when you are revealing and assaulting the Rebel base, or when you need to remove Rebel units to subjugate a key system. For the Rebels, combat matters when you need to fulfill an objective that requires winning a battle, or when you are defending the revealed base. Avoid unnecessary engagements that drain your limited resources.

walkthrough.title: Probe Card Management for the Empire
walkthrough.text: Probe cards are the Empire's most important tool for finding the base. You draw two per Refresh Phase, but that is not fast enough on its own. Use the Gather Intel and Long Range Probe missions to accelerate your search. Keep a mental map of which systems you have eliminated. Cross-reference with Rebel behavior: if the Rebel player avoids moving units through certain areas, the base might be nearby. Pay attention to which systems they gain loyalty in and where they deploy units. Every piece of information helps narrow the search.

walkthrough.title: Rebel Base Placement Strategy
walkthrough.text: Where you place the base defines your entire game. Avoid placing it in your starting loyal systems — the Empire will check those first. Remote systems cannot be the base because they have no loyalty space, but neighboring systems are often overlooked. Consider systems far from Imperial starting positions with at least one resource icon for building. Some Rebel players place the base near the edge of the board where there are fewer adjacent systems for the Empire to approach from. The best base locations are ones the Empire would not think to check early.

summary.title: Common Mistakes for Both Sides
bullets: Empire: guessing base without evidence, ignoring objectives, spreading too thin
Rebels: obvious base placement, leaving movement trails, hoarding objectives
Both: overcommitting leaders to missions and leaving the pool empty

summary.title: Imperial Opening Priorities
bullets: Subjugate systems early — more resources = more units each build phase
Use intel missions to draw probe cards and narrow base search
Position forces to reach multiple suspected locations quickly
Be patient — Empire's advantage grows each round

summary.title: Rebel Opening Priorities
bullets: Diplomacy missions for loyalty — fuels building and objectives
Use safe Resolve missions to avoid dice risk when possible
Create decoy presence in 1-2 systems; avoid revealing base through movement
Plan around objective cards — every action should aim toward reputation gains

summary.title: Leader Assignment Strategy
bullets: Assign to missions only with strong targets and skill matches
Keep at least 1 leader with tactic values in pool for activation/response
Vader: great on spec ops missions AND in combat; Leia: diplomacy missions AND opposition
Never assign all 4 leaders — you need pool flexibility

summary.title: Understand the Time Pressure
bullets: Time marker advances 1/round; reputation starts at 14; ~14 rounds max
Good Rebel play closes the gap fast — Empire must slow reputation gains
Empire alarm: reputation at 10 with no base leads = trouble
Rebel alarm: no reputation by round 4 = need to take more risks

summary.title: Combat Is a Means, Not an End
bullets: Combat drains leaders, units, and tactic cards — fight only for strategic gain
Empire: combat for base assault or clearing systems for subjugation
Rebels: combat for battle-dependent objectives or defending revealed base
Avoid unnecessary engagements that waste limited resources

summary.title: Probe Card Management for the Empire
bullets: Draw 2 probe cards per Refresh Phase — supplement with intel missions
Track eliminated systems mentally; cross-reference with Rebel behavior
Watch where Rebels gain loyalty, deploy units, and avoid moving
Gather Intel and Long Range Probe accelerate the search

summary.title: Rebel Base Placement Strategy
bullets: Avoid starting loyal systems — Empire checks those first
Consider systems far from Imperial starting positions
Edge-of-board systems have fewer approach routes
Best base: somewhere the Empire would not think to check early

walkthrough.title: Imperial Early Game: The Subjugation Rush
walkthrough.text: The strongest Imperial opening prioritizes rapid subjugation over base hunting. Use General Tagge to activate neutral systems and drop Stormtroopers. Each subjugated system adds one resource icon to your build economy. By round 3, if you have subjugated three or four additional systems beyond your starting five, you are building significantly more units than the Rebels. This production snowball is the Empire's core advantage. Meanwhile, use Yularen's intel to run Gather Intel missions and draw probe cards passively. Do not commit to a base assault until you have eliminated at least half the possible locations.

walkthrough.title: Imperial Mid Game: The Constriction
walkthrough.text: Once you have a strong economic base, shift to constricting the Rebels. Use diplomacy missions to flip Rebel loyalty, removing their ability to build and deploy. Position fleets in systems adjacent to your remaining suspected base locations so you can strike quickly when you identify the base. Keep probe card pressure up. The ideal mid-game Imperial position is one where the Rebels have few loyal systems, limited units, and you have three or four candidate base systems under surveillance. The Rebels become desperate and make mistakes when they feel the noose tightening.

walkthrough.title: Imperial Endgame: The Base Assault
walkthrough.text: When you have narrowed the base to one or two systems, it is time to strike. Send your heaviest fleet with your best combat leader. Bring Vader or Palpatine for their high tactic values to draw maximum tactic cards. Overwhelming force is critical — the Rebels will have concentrated their forces at the base. Remember, you need to land ground units in the system to reveal the base, and then destroy all Rebel units there. Bring AT-ATs and AT-STs for ground combat punch. If the Rebel player has a Shield Generator or Ion Cannon, expect a tough ground fight. The Death Star can destroy the system, but only with the Superlaser Online mission.

walkthrough.title: Rebel Early Game: Objective Pipeline
walkthrough.text: The Rebel player's early game should focus entirely on creating a pipeline for scoring objectives. Read your starting objective card carefully and work backward from its requirements. If it needs Rebel loyalty in systems, use diplomacy missions with Mon Mothma and Leia to gain loyalty. If it needs combat victories, position a small strike force. Draw your objective cards each Refresh Phase and evaluate which ones are achievable in the near term. Stage I objectives are generally easier — do not hoard them waiting for Stage III cards. Score early and often. Two reputation from a Stage I objective is worth more than a hypothetical five from a Stage III card you might never fulfill.

walkthrough.title: Rebel Mid Game: Misdirection and Mobility
walkthrough.text: By mid-game, the Empire has narrowed the base search. Your job is to create confusion. Move units from the Rebel Base space to one system, then activate in a completely different area. Make the Empire think the base is somewhere it is not. If you have the Rapid Mobilization starting mission, consider using it to relocate the base to an area the Empire has already searched and dismissed. Use hit-and-run tactics with fast ships to force the Empire to react rather than probe. Every round the Empire spends chasing phantoms is a round you get closer to winning. Keep scoring objectives whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Rebel Endgame: The Final Push
walkthrough.text: When the reputation marker is within four or five spaces of the time marker, every decision is life or death. Look for any objective you can score immediately. The Death Star Plans objective card is often a game-winner: if the Empire has deployed a Death Star, you only need one fighter in the system and one direct hit on three dice. Use the One in a Million action card (if you have it) to guarantee the direct hit. Even if you cannot destroy the Death Star, winning a space battle near it can fulfill other objectives. In the final rounds, consider revealing your base voluntarily if it forces the Empire to redirect forces, buying you one more round to score.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Leader Tactics: The Vader Gambit
walkthrough.text: Darth Vader is the Empire's most versatile leader. He has spec ops icons for capture missions and strong tactic values for combat. An advanced technique is to assign Vader to a capture mission targeting a key Rebel leader, then not reveal it immediately. Keep him on the mission card as a threat — the Rebel player has to account for the possibility that Vader will capture one of their leaders. Meanwhile, use your other leaders to activate systems. If the Rebel player overcommits to oppose a different mission, reveal Vader's capture mission elsewhere where there is no opposition. The threat of Vader is often more valuable than actually using him.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Leader Tactics: Rebel Leader Synergy
walkthrough.text: Princess Leia and Mon Mothma are the Rebel backbone. Leia has diplomacy and intel icons, making her ideal for loyalty missions and opposing Imperial intel missions. Mon Mothma is a pure diplomat who can gain loyalty in multiple systems through missions. Use them together on a diplomacy mission for maximum dice, or split them to cover two missions in one round. Jan Dodonna is your combat leader with strong tactic values — save him for when you need to fight. General Rieekan is flexible and great for activating systems. Once you recruit Luke Skywalker or Han Solo, your options expand dramatically. Luke Jedi has powerful spec ops and combat icons.

walkthrough.title: The Action Card Meta
walkthrough.text: Action cards are some of the most powerful effects in the game, and knowing which ones exist changes your strategy. For the Empire, the Son of Skywalker card can convert Luke to the dark side if the conditions are right, eliminating a key Rebel leader. The Imperial March card lets you move units during the Assignment Phase, a devastating timing advantage. For the Rebels, One in a Million guarantees a Death Star Plans success. The Plan of Attack card can let you surprise the Empire with unexpected combat. Keep track of which action cards your opponent might have based on which leaders they recruited. If they recruited a specific leader, they likely have that leader's action card.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Build Economy
walkthrough.text: Whoever controls more resource icons builds more units. The Empire starts ahead and can widen the gap through subjugation. Advanced Imperial play targets specific high-value systems that have multiple resource icons, giving maximum return per subjugation action. The Rebels can fight back with the Sabotage mission, which places a sabotage marker preventing a system from building or deploying. Sabotaging a system with two or three resource icons is devastating to Imperial production. Timing sabotage before a build phase maximizes its impact. The economic war often determines the military balance of the mid and late game.

summary.title: Imperial Early Game: The Subjugation Rush
bullets: Subjugate 3-4 neutral systems by round 3 for production snowball
Use Tagge to activate and drop Stormtroopers in neutral systems
Run Gather Intel passively with Yularen to draw probe cards
Do not commit to base assault until 50%+ of locations eliminated

summary.title: Imperial Mid Game: The Constriction
bullets: Flip Rebel loyalty with diplomacy missions — remove their build/deploy ability
Position fleets adjacent to suspected base locations for rapid response
Maintain probe card pressure — narrow candidates to 3-4 systems
Force Rebels into desperation moves by tightening the noose

summary.title: Imperial Endgame: The Base Assault
bullets: Strike with heaviest fleet + best combat leader (Vader/Palpatine tactic values)
Must land ground units to reveal base, then destroy all Rebel units
Bring AT-ATs/AT-STs for ground punch; expect Shield Generators/Ion Cannons
Death Star can destroy system via Superlaser Online mission

summary.title: Rebel Early Game: Objective Pipeline
bullets: Read objectives and work backward from requirements
Use Leia and Mon Mothma for diplomacy missions to gain loyalty
Score Stage I objectives early — don't hoard for hypothetical Stage III cards
Two reputation now beats five reputation never

summary.title: Rebel Mid Game: Misdirection and Mobility
bullets: Move units from Rebel Base to decoy systems; activate in different areas
Rapid Mobilization can relocate base to already-searched area
Hit-and-run with fast ships to force Empire to react instead of probe
Every wasted Imperial round = Rebels closer to winning

summary.title: Rebel Endgame: The Final Push
bullets: When reputation is within 4-5 of time marker: every action counts
Death Star Plans: 1 fighter in system + 3 dice + 1 direct hit = DS destroyed + reputation
One in a Million action card guarantees the direct hit if available
Consider voluntary base reveal to redirect Empire forces and buy time

summary.title: Advanced Leader Tactics: The Vader Gambit
summary.text: Assign Vader to a capture mission but delay revealing it. The threat forces the Rebel player to hold back leaders for opposition. If they overcommit elsewhere, reveal Vader's mission unopposed. The threat of Vader is often more valuable than using him immediately.
bullets: Assign Vader to capture mission but delay reveal as a threat
Rebels must hold back leaders to oppose potential capture
If Rebels overcommit elsewhere, reveal Vader's mission unopposed
The threat of capture is often more valuable than the capture itself

summary.title: Advanced Leader Tactics: Rebel Leader Synergy
bullets: Leia: diplomacy + intel — loyalty missions and opposing Imperial intel
Mon Mothma: pure diplomat — multiple loyalty missions per game
Dodonna: combat leader with strong tactic values — save for key fights
Luke Jedi and Han Solo dramatically expand Rebel options when recruited

summary.title: The Action Card Meta
bullets: Son of Skywalker: can convert Luke to dark side — devastating if it lands
Imperial March: move units during Assignment Phase — timing advantage
One in a Million: guarantees Death Star Plans success for Rebels
Track opponent's recruited leaders to predict which action cards they hold

summary.title: Controlling the Build Economy
bullets: Target high-value systems with multiple resource icons for subjugation
Rebel Sabotage mission prevents building and deploying from a system
Time sabotage before build phases for maximum impact
The economic war determines the military balance of mid-late game

entries.definition: The first phase of each game round. Players assign leaders from their leader pools to facedown mission cards. The Rebel player assigns first, then the Imperial player. Leaders not assigned remain in the pool for the Command Phase.

The second phase of each game round. Players alternate turns activating systems, revealing missions, or passing. The Rebel player takes the first turn. Once a player passes, they cannot take more turns but can still oppose missions and add leaders to combat.

The third phase of each game round, with six steps: retrieve leaders, draw missions, launch probe droids (Imperial draws 2 probe cards), draw objective (Rebel draws 1), advance time marker (recruit/build if icons shown), and deploy units from the build queue.

A Command Phase action. Place a leader with tactic values from your pool into any system. Then move units from adjacent systems into it. If Imperial ground units enter, the Rebel base may be revealed. Combat occurs if both factions have units in the same theater.

A Command Phase action. Flip an assigned mission card faceup. Place assigned leaders in the target system. If the mission says Attempt, the opponent can oppose it with dice rolls. If it says Resolve, the ability happens automatically.

A Command Phase action. End your Command Phase turns for the round. You can still send leaders from your pool to oppose missions or add them to combat, but you cannot activate systems or reveal missions.

A mission keyword indicating the mission can be opposed. Both players roll dice equal to their leaders' matching skill icons. The attempter needs more successes than the opponent. Hits and direct hits are 1 success each; specials are 2 successes.

A mission keyword indicating the mission cannot be opposed. When revealed, the ability is performed immediately without dice rolls. Resolve missions are safer but generally less powerful than Attempt missions.

Each faction has 4 starting missions identified by a yellow arrow at the bottom. These can never be discarded and return to the player's hand after being used. They form the backbone of each faction's strategy.

Special Imperial mission cards kept in their own deck on the Projects space. The Imperial player draws them by resolving the Research and Development mission. They include powerful abilities like Construct Death Star and Superlaser Online.

When an Attempt mission is revealed, the opponent may send one leader from their pool to the system. All leaders in the system with matching skill icons contribute dice. The opposition succeeds if the attempter does not get more successes.

One of four leader skill types. Diplomacy missions often provide loyalty in systems or give players additional units. Leaders with diplomacy icons include Princess Leia and Mon Mothma (Rebel) and Emperor Palpatine (Imperial).

One of four leader skill types. Rebel intel missions help acquire objective cards. Imperial intel missions help find the Rebel base by drawing probe cards. Leaders with intel icons include Leia (Rebel) and Colonel Yularen (Imperial).

One of four leader skill types. Rebel logistics missions typically move units to and from the base. Imperial logistics missions build special units or accelerate production. Key logistics leaders include General Rieekan (Rebel) and General Tagge (Imperial).

One of four leader skill types. Rebel spec ops missions generally destroy Imperial units. Imperial spec ops missions capture Rebel leaders. Key spec ops leaders include Darth Vader (Imperial) and recruited leaders like Han Solo and Luke Skywalker (Rebel).

The space on each faction sheet where unassigned leaders are kept. Leaders in the pool can be assigned to missions, used to activate systems, sent to oppose missions, or added to combat. Leaders return here during Refresh Phase step 1.

Numbers on a leader's stand indicating how many space and ground tactic cards are drawn at the start of combat. A leader must have at least one tactic value to activate a system. Multiple leaders in combat use the highest value per theater.
Imperial starting leader. Strong diplomacy and spec ops icons. High tactic values make him powerful in combat. One of the Empire's most versatile leaders for both missions and battles.
Imperial starting leader. Excels at spec ops missions, particularly capture missions targeting Rebel leaders. Strong tactic values for combat. Often the most feared Imperial leader on the board.
Imperial starting leader. Logistics specialist used for activating systems and subjugating neutral territory. Important for early-game expansion and building the Empire's economic engine.
Imperial starting leader. Intel specialist used for probe-drawing missions like Gather Intel. Key to the Empire's search for the Rebel base through information gathering.
Rebel starting leader. Versatile with diplomacy and intel icons. Used for gaining loyalty in systems and opposing Imperial intel missions. One of the Rebellion's most valuable mission leaders.
Rebel starting leader. Pure diplomat focused on gaining loyalty in systems. Her diplomacy icons make her ideal for loyalty missions that support Rebel objectives and building capacity.
Rebel starting leader. Logistics specialist useful for activating systems and moving units. Flexible enough to handle multiple roles when other leaders are assigned to missions.
Rebel starting leader. The Rebellion's primary combat leader with strong tactic values. Best used for opposing missions or leading battles when combat is unavoidable.
Recruitable Rebel leader. Gained through action card recruitment. Has a Jedi version with powerful spec ops and combat icons. Key to many Rebel strategies and action card abilities.
Recruitable Rebel leader. Versatile with spec ops icons. Often paired with Chewbacca for complementary abilities. Important for aggressive Rebel strategies and special missions.

Occurs when both factions have units in the same theater in a system. Resolved in rounds: add leaders, draw tactic cards, then space battle, ground battle, retreat, and check for continuation. Ends when one side has no units in a contested theater.

Step I of each combat round. The current player attacks with all ships, then the opponent attacks with all ships. Only resolved if both factions have ships in the system. Damage is assigned only to ships.

Step II of each combat round. The current player attacks with all ground units, then the opponent attacks with all ground units. Only resolved if both factions have ground units in the system. Damage is assigned only to ground units.

Drawn during combat based on leader tactic values. Space tactic cards for space battles, ground tactic cards for ground battles. Can block damage, deal extra damage, or provide special effects. Shuffled back into their decks at end of combat.

A die result that deals 1 damage. Red die hits can only damage units with red health values. Black die hits can only damage units with black health values. Also counts as 1 success during mission attempts.
A die result that deals 1 damage to a unit of any health color. More flexible than regular hits. Also counts as 1 success during mission attempts.
A die result that can be spent to draw a tactic card or play a tactic card with a special icon requirement. During missions, a special result counts as 2 successes.

Placed under a unit to track damage that does not destroy it. Damage persists across combat rounds but is removed at the end of combat. A unit is destroyed when its damage equals or exceeds its health value.

Step III of each combat round. Move one leader and your units to an adjacent friendly system. All ships must leave; ground units and TIE Fighters may stay. Cannot retreat to enemy systems or the system the attacker came from. Empire cannot retreat with a Death Star.

Ships provide transport capacity shown on the faction sheet. Each ground unit or TIE Fighter requires 1 capacity to move. Units with the transport restriction icon can only move when ships with capacity are also moving from the same system.
Units with the immobile icon cannot move. This includes the Death Star Under Construction and Rebel structures (Shield Generator, Ion Cannon). They stay in their system permanently unless destroyed.
Two systems that share a border on the game board. Units can move between adjacent systems. Region borders (orange) can be crossed, but impassable areas (light red) cannot be moved through.
Imperial fighter ship. Black health value, 1 attack die. Requires transport capacity to move. Cheap and numerous, they provide screening and fighter coverage. 24 included in the game.
Imperial ship with transport capacity. Carries ground units and TIE Fighters into battle. 8 included in the game. Important for projecting ground force across the galaxy.
Imperial capital ship. Red health value with strong attack values in both black and red dice. The backbone of the Imperial fleet. 8 included in the game. Provides transport capacity.

The most powerful conventional Imperial ship. Massive attack values and health. 2 included in the game. Built from triple resource icons, so they take significant investment but dominate space combat.

Imperial space station. Cannot be damaged through normal combat. The Empire cannot retreat when a Death Star is in combat. Can destroy an entire system with the Superlaser Online mission. 2 included in the game.
An immobile precursor to a completed Death Star. Created by the Construct Death Star mission. If destroyed, the completed Death Star on the build queue is also destroyed. 1 included in the game.
Imperial ground unit. The basic infantry of the Empire. Used for occupying systems, subjugation, and ground combat. 30 included in the game. Essential for controlling territory.
Imperial ground unit. Stronger than Stormtroopers with better attack values. 10 included in the game. Provide additional ground combat firepower for system assaults.

Imperial ground unit. The Empire's most powerful ground combat unit with high attack values and health. 4 included in the game. Critical for assaulting well-defended Rebel positions and the Rebel base.
Rebel fighter ship. Black health value with strong attack capabilities. The Rebellion's primary space combat unit. 8 included in the game. Required for Death Star Plans objective.
Rebel fighter ship. Provides bombing capability with red dice attacks. 12 included in the game. Important for dealing damage to capital ships in space combat.
Rebel capital ship. Provides transport capacity for moving ground units. 4 included in the game. Essential for Rebel force projection and retreating ground forces.
Rebel ship providing transport capacity. 4 included in the game. If Rebel Transports are the only remaining Rebel ships in a combat, they must retreat or are immediately destroyed.
Rebel capital ship. The Rebellion's most powerful ship with strong attack values and high health. 3 included in the game. The Mon Cal Cruiser is the Rebel answer to Star Destroyers.
Rebel ground unit. The basic infantry of the Rebellion. 21 included in the game. Used for defending systems and the Rebel base in ground combat.
Rebel ground unit. Faster and more mobile than Rebel Troopers with better attack values. 6 included in the game. Provide additional ground combat capability.
Rebel structure (ground unit). Immobile. Provides a defensive ability in the system where it is placed, helping protect Rebel ground forces. 3 included in the game. Key for base defense.
Rebel structure (ground unit). Immobile. Provides an ability that affects enemy ships in the system. 3 included in the game. Combined with Shield Generator, creates strong base defenses.

Indicates which faction a system's population supports. Loyal systems provide resources for building units and allow unit deployment. Gained through missions. Coruscant is permanently Imperial. Each system holds at most one loyalty marker.

Occurs when Imperial ground units occupy a system without Imperial loyalty. A subjugation marker is placed, allowing the Empire to build from the first resource icon and deploy there. Removed if all Imperial ground units leave the system.
A system with no loyalty marker or subjugation marker. Neither faction can build from or deploy to neutral systems. They can be subjugated by Imperial ground units or gain loyalty through missions.
A system with at least one resource icon and a loyalty space. Most systems on the board are populous. They can have loyalty markers and contribute to unit production.
A system without resource icons or a loyalty space. Cannot have loyalty or subjugation markers. Units cannot be deployed there. They serve as transit points and hiding places.

Icons in populous systems indicating what unit types can be built there during the Refresh Phase. Blue icons build ships; orange icons build ground units. The number next to each icon indicates which build queue space to place the unit on.
The game board has 8 regions separated by thick orange borders, each containing 4 systems. Units can move across region borders. Some card abilities affect all systems in a region.

A system destroyed by the Superlaser Online mission. A destroyed system marker is placed. All Rebel ground units are destroyed. Imperial ground units may be destroyed if they exceed transport capacity. No ground battles or loyalty can occur in destroyed systems.
The Imperial capital. Always loyal to the Empire and cannot gain or lose loyalty. Not included in the probe deck. A critical resource system for Imperial production throughout the game.

The hidden Rebel headquarters. Secretly placed during setup using a probe card. While hidden, Rebel units can move to and from the Rebel Base space. Revealed when Imperial ground units enter the system or voluntarily by the Rebel player.

The labeled space on the game board, separate from the actual base system. Units can be built, deployed, and stored here while the base is hidden. Not a system — missions cannot be attempted here unless specified.

One card per system (except Coruscant). Used to determine starting loyalty and to secretly choose the Rebel base location. The Imperial player draws 2 per Refresh Phase, eliminating possible base locations through process of elimination.

Rebel cards that grant reputation when their conditions are fulfilled. Organized in Stages I, II, and III of increasing difficulty and reward. Played during combat or at Start of Refresh Phase. One drawn per Refresh Phase. No hand limit.

The Rebel victory tracker. Starts at space 14 on the time track and moves toward the time marker when the Rebel player gains reputation from objectives. If the reputation marker reaches the time marker, the Rebels win immediately.

Advances one space each Refresh Phase along the time track. Spaces contain recruit and build icons that trigger during step 5 of the Refresh Phase. If the time marker reaches the reputation marker, the Rebels win.

Each player's production pipeline on their faction sheet. Units are placed at numbered spaces when built. Each Refresh Phase, all units slide one space down. Units that slide off the end are deployed to loyal or subjugated systems.

Powerful one-time ability cards tied to specific leaders. Gained during setup (starting action cards) and when recruiting leaders. Used once then returned to the game box. Timing keywords: Assignment, Start of Combat, Immediate, Special.

A Rebel leader captured by an Imperial mission. The captured ring is attached. Captured leaders cannot return to the leader pool, oppose missions, or be moved by the Rebel player. Rescued leaders go to the Rebel Base space.

Placed by the Rebel Sabotage mission. Prevents the system from building units or deploying units during the Refresh Phase. Stays on the board until removed by an Imperial mission. Affects both factions equally.
A ship with a red health value. Includes Star Destroyers, Super Star Destroyers, Corellian Corvettes, and Mon Calamari Cruisers. The Death Star is a space station, not a capital ship.
A ship with a black health value. Includes TIE Fighters, X-wings, and Y-wings. TIE Fighters require transport capacity to move. Rebel fighters needed for Death Star Plans objective.

Rebel ground units that are immobile and provide ongoing defensive benefits. Includes Shield Generator and Ion Cannon. Multiple structures of the same type can be in one system, each providing its benefit.

The Death Star and Death Star Under Construction are space stations. They are ships but not capital ships or fighters. The Death Star cannot be damaged normally; the Under Construction version is immobile.
The game has two theaters: space and ground. Ships fight in space battles; ground units fight in ground battles. Damage can only be assigned to units in the same theater being resolved.

A ring attached to a leader's stand providing an ongoing effect, either beneficial or detrimental. A leader can have only one ring at a time. If a leader gains a second ring, the first is removed. The captured ring is the most common.

Rebel starting mission that allows establishing a new base. Draw 4 probe cards and optionally choose a new base location. Resolved at the end of the Command Phase. One of the most powerful Rebel tools for survival.

Imperial starting mission. An Attempt mission using intel icons. When successful, the Imperial player draws probe cards based on Rebel units in the system. Critical for narrowing down the base location.

Imperial project mission that can destroy an entire system using the Death Star. Places a destroyed system marker and eliminates all Rebel ground units. One of the most devastating effects in the game.
Imperial project mission that builds a second Death Star. Places a Death Star Under Construction in the mission's system and a completed Death Star on space 3 of the build queue.

Rebel objective card. During space battle, if the Rebel player has at least 1 fighter in the system, roll 3 dice. If at least 1 direct hit is rolled, the Death Star is destroyed and the Rebel player gains reputation.
Rebel mission that places a sabotage marker in a system, preventing it from building or deploying units. A powerful economic disruption tool targeting high-value Imperial production systems.
